principles.label: Robotics as the Ultimate Reality Check
principles.summary: Building AI tied into physical systems is the most natural path to general intelligence, as physical embodiment quickly reveals the true capabilities and limitations of algorithms that might otherwise overfit to simple simulators.
principles.details: Natural intelligence in the real world is tied to physical embodiment. Testing algorithms on physical robots exposes the gap between simulated progress and real-world utility.
principles.representative_quote: In the real world, the natural intelligence is all in physical embodiment. It seemed to me the most natural place to start to try to build AI is tied into physical systems; tied into robots, is a more natural way to make progress.

principles.label: Software 2.0: Data Over Hard-Coded Rules
principles.summary: Programming is shifting from writing explicit lines of code to curating data. Hard-coding rules requires endless exceptions to handle edge cases, which eventually becomes unwieldy and fragile in the real world.
principles.details: Traditional programming fails at complex perception tasks. Deep learning succeeds by learning directly from labeled data, scaling much better than 'if-then-else' logic.
principles.representative_quote: Once you start putting in rules, then you have to put, another 'if then else', another 'if then else', and at some point, things become unwieldy.

principles.label: AI as a Meta-Discipline
principles.summary: Working on artificial intelligence is a way to work on everything, because AI serves as a foundational tool that can accelerate progress across all other disciplines.
principles.details: Intelligence is what sets humans apart. By building intelligent tools, researchers can have a global impact on fields ranging from biology to physics.
principles.representative_quote: Maybe by working on artificial intelligence, in some way, I could be working on everything, because maybe AI could help everything else.

principles.label: Deep Learning as the Path to AGI
principles.summary: Deep learning has driven every major high-impact breakthrough in AI over the last decade and remains the most viable, productive path toward solving open challenges and achieving Artificial General Intelligence.
principles.details: Because the brain is essentially storage and compute, an artificial system with sufficient compute, storage, sensors, and actuators can achieve intelligence. Deep learning is the architecture best suited to leverage this compute.
principles.representative_quote: In the last 10 years, every single breakthrough that has been of high impact in AI has been deep learning. There's literally been no exception that I'm aware of.

principles.label: Bootstrapping Real-World RL
principles.summary: Real-world AI deployment should bootstrap with human behavioral cloning before applying reinforcement learning, as pure RL from scratch is too slow and unsafe.
principles.details: Reinforcement learning requires experiencing negative outcomes to learn. In real-world applications, it is safer and faster to start with supervised learning from human demonstrations and fuse in RL only once the system is highly capable.

principles.label: Sim2Real via Domain Randomization
principles.summary: Simulation is necessary for scaling robotics, but because simulators cannot perfectly match reality, models must be trained across massive simulated variations to transfer successfully to the real world.
principles.details: By exposing the model to randomized physical parameters (friction, mass, lighting) in simulation, it learns robust features that allow it to treat the real world as just another variation.
principles.representative_quote: If the model sees enough simulated variation, the real world may look like just the next simulator

principles.label: Human Preferences for Reward Design
principles.summary: For complex tasks where manually defining a reward function is difficult, putting humans in the loop to provide binary preferences between behaviors prevents reward exploitation.
principles.details: Manual reward functions often lead to agents finding unintended loopholes. Human preference learning allows the system to learn an accurate, nuanced reward model directly from non-expert feedback.
principles.representative_quote: Putting (non-expert) humans into the agent learning loop!

principles.label: Probabilistic Reasoning & Optimization as Foundations
principles.summary: State-of-the-art robotic systems and modern Artificial Intelligence are fundamentally built on the mathematics of probabilistic reasoning and optimization.
principles.details: Mastering these two mathematical areas is essential because they form the core algorithms of robotics and have wide applicability across all of AI.
principles.representative_quote: The majority of these techniques are heavily based on probabilistic reasoning and optimization---two areas with wide applicability in modern Artificial Intelligence.

principles.label: Adaptive Policies over Perfect Models
principles.summary: In model-based reinforcement learning, instead of trying to learn a single perfect dynamics model, it is better to learn an adaptive policy over an ensemble of imperfect models.
principles.details: Because learned models are always imperfect, policies optimized on a single model perform well in simulation but fail in reality. An adaptive policy trained across an ensemble can quickly adapt to how the real world actually works.
principles.representative_quote: Learn an ***adaptive policy*** that can quickly adapt to any of the learned models

principles.label: Hardware Designed for AI
principles.summary: Robotic hardware should be designed with the assumption that it will be controlled by intelligent AI using visual and force feedback, rather than rigid, pre-programmed geometric paths.
principles.details: Future robots will operate much like humans do, requiring hardware that supports dynamic, feedback-driven control rather than just high-precision, blind repetition.
principles.representative_quote: This robot is designed for the assumption that in the future, robots will be controlled much more intelligently by AI systems that use visual feedback, that use force feedback, much like how humans control their own arms.

frameworks.label: Domain Randomization
frameworks.summary: A method for training robust neural networks in simulation that can successfully deploy to the real world without requiring a perfect simulator.
frameworks.details: Steps: 1. Build many versions of the simulator. 2. Randomize physical parameters (friction, mass, camera position) rather than trying to match reality. 3. Train a single neural network to control the robot across all simulators. 4. Deploy to the real world, which the network treats as just another variation.

frameworks.label: Hindsight Experience Replay
frameworks.summary: A technique for training reinforcement learning agents in sparse-reward environments by pretending that whatever outcome the agent accidentally achieved was its intended goal.
frameworks.details: Steps: 1. Allow the robot to attempt a task. 2. Observe the actual outcome (e.g., falling down). 3. Pretend the achieved outcome was the intended goal and reward the robot. 4. Internalize these 'accidental' skills to build a broad repertoire that makes learning the actual target task easier.

frameworks.label: Bootstrapped Reinforcement Learning Deployment
frameworks.summary: A pragmatic pipeline for deploying AI agents in real-world scenarios where safety and time are critical constraints.
frameworks.details: Steps: 1. Start with supervised learning and behavioral cloning where humans do the work. 2. Have the ML system match human actions and make suggestions. 3. Once highly capable, gradually fuse in reinforcement learning by providing actual achievement objectives.

frameworks.label: Learning Reward from Preferences
frameworks.summary: A framework for solving the reward design problem by putting non-expert humans in the agent learning loop.
frameworks.details: Steps: 1. Collect samples via interactions with the environment. 2. Collect human preferences by showing two segments and getting a binary label. 3. Optimize a reward model using cross entropy loss. 4. Optimize a policy using off-policy algorithms. Repeat.
frameworks.representative_quote: Step 1. Collect samples via interactions with environment Step 2. Collect human preferences Step 3. Optimize a reward model using cross entropy loss Step 4. Optimize a policy using off-policy algorithms

frameworks.label: Model-Based RL via Meta Policy Optimization (MB-MPO)
frameworks.summary: A method to overcome model-bias and training instability in model-based RL by learning an adaptive policy over an ensemble of simulators.
frameworks.details: Steps: 1. Collect data under current adaptive policies. 2. Learn an ENSEMBLE of K simulators from all past data. 3. Perform meta-policy optimization over the ENSEMBLE. 4. Generate new meta-policy and new adaptive policies.
frameworks.representative_quote: for iter = 1, 2, … collect data under current adaptive policies, learn ENSEMBLE of K simulators from all past data, meta-policy optimization over ENSEMBLE

mental_models.label: Unsupervised RL as 'Play'
mental_models.summary: Viewing a robot's unsupervised exploration of its environment as analogous to a child playing, building a foundation of generalized knowledge that accelerates future task learning.
mental_models.details: Instead of optimizing for a specific task, the agent is rewarded for curiosity. It tries a wide range of random interactions with its own body and environment without a specific goal, much like a child exploring the world before being taught specific chores.
mental_models.representative_quote: If you transpose this onto humans, think of it as play. When you have children, they just play. They explore the world by play.

mental_models.label: The Research vs. Real-World Gap
mental_models.summary: The threshold for success is fundamentally different between academia (proving a concept works) and industry (near-perfect reliability across a massive distribution of edge cases).
mental_models.details: In research, achieving 70% on a benchmark means you move on to the next one. In the real world, even 90% is not enough because of the long tail of scenarios.

mental_models.label: Meta-Learning (Learning the Algorithm)
mental_models.summary: Treating the reinforcement learning algorithm itself as a program that can be optimized by an outer loop, rather than manually designing complex details of exploration and credit assignment.
mental_models.details: Run reinforcement learning in the 'inner loop' and use an outer program to modify and improve the inner algorithm based on its learning speed.

mental_models.label: Model-Bias (Overfitting in Model-based RL)
mental_models.summary: A specific type of overfitting where policy optimization exploits regions of the state space where there is insufficient data to train the dynamics model accurately, leading to catastrophic failures.
mental_models.details: Unlike standard supervised learning overfitting, this occurs because the agent actively seeks out the 'blind spots' of its own internal model where it falsely believes it can achieve high rewards.
mental_models.representative_quote: policy optimization tends to exploit regions where insufficient data is available to train the model, leading to catastrophic failures

mental_models.label: The Single Brain Model
mental_models.summary: The concept that future AI systems will use a single, unified neural network to process both passive observation (like internet videos) and active physical experience (reinforcement learning).
mental_models.details: Mirroring human cognition, a robot uses the same neural network to learn how to chop carrots by watching YouTube videos and to learn how to physically move its own robotic hands through trial and error.

mental_models.label: The AI Progress Engine
mental_models.summary: AI advances through a combination of fundamental conceptual breakthroughs compounded by massive amounts of incremental improvements, all gated by the availability of compute and data.
mental_models.details: Early neural nets failed not due to a lack of smart researchers, but because the research cycle was too slow without sufficient compute to run experiments and iterate.

heuristics.label: Randomize the Simulator
heuristics.summary: If you can't perfectly match a simulator to reality, randomize the simulator's parameters instead.
heuristics.representative_quote: If the model sees enough simulated variation, the real world may look like just the next simulator

heuristics.label: Bootstrap RL with Imitation
heuristics.summary: Bootstrap reinforcement learning through imitation: use supervised learning on human demonstrations to initialize the policy before letting it explore.

heuristics.label: Robotics as a Reality Check
heuristics.summary: Use robotics as a reality check for AI progress to ensure algorithms aren't just overfitting to simple simulators.
heuristics.representative_quote: It's very easy to think you make a lot of progress, and then you try to see if a real robot can do something in a real environment that you quickly realize that the results weren’t great

heuristics.label: Deep Networks for Patterns
heuristics.summary: If there is a pattern in the data, assume we can probably represent it with a deep network.
heuristics.representative_quote: If there is a pattern we can probably represent it with a deep network and capture that pattern.

heuristics.label: Focused Literature Reviews
heuristics.summary: When writing a research paper, only survey prior work that is directly relevant to understanding your specific approach.
heuristics.details: Focus primarily on the problem setting, novelty, approach, and results.

signature_quotes.label: Physical Embodiment
signature_quotes.summary: On why robotics is the natural starting point for general intelligence.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: In the real world, the natural intelligence is all in physical embodiment. It seemed to me the most natural place to start to try to build AI is tied into physical systems; tied into robots.

signature_quotes.label: Simulated Variation
signature_quotes.summary: On the core concept of Domain Randomization for Sim2Real transfer.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: If the model sees enough simulated variation, the real world may look like just the next simulator

signature_quotes.label: AI as Everything
signature_quotes.summary: On choosing to dedicate his career to artificial intelligence.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: Maybe by working on artificial intelligence, in some way, I could be working on everything, because maybe AI could help everything else.

signature_quotes.label: Software 2.0 Paradigm
signature_quotes.summary: On the fundamental shift brought by deep learning.

signature_quotes.label: The Joy and Danger of RL
signature_quotes.summary: On the impracticality of pure reinforcement learning for immediate real-world deployment.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: Very few things more fun to watch than a reinforcement learning robot starting from nothing and inventing things. But it's just time consuming and it's not always safe.

signature_quotes.label: Deep Learning Dominance
signature_quotes.summary: On the undeniable track record of deep learning in recent AI history.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: In the last 10 years, every single breakthrough that has been of high impact in AI has been deep learning. There's literally been no exception that I'm aware of.

signature_quotes.label: Mathematical Foundations
signature_quotes.summary: On the core math required to understand state-of-the-art robotic systems.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: The majority of these techniques are heavily based on probabilistic reasoning and optimization---two areas with wide applicability in modern Artificial Intelligence.

anti_patterns.label: Hard-Coding Rules and Assumptions
anti_patterns.summary: Building assumptions or 'if-then-else' statements directly into AI systems to solve a single problem.
anti_patterns.details: The real world has too many exceptions. Adding rules requires adding endless exceptions, which eventually makes the system unwieldy, fragile, and incapable of generalizing to unforeseen situations.
anti_patterns.representative_quote: Once you start putting in rules, then you have to put, another 'if then else', another 'if then else', and at some point, things become unwieldy.

anti_patterns.label: Perfecting the Simulator
anti_patterns.summary: Trying to perfectly match a simulation to reality for Sim2Real transfer.
anti_patterns.details: It is typically impossible to get a perfect match. If the simulation is even slightly off, a neural network trained purely in that simulation will likely fail when deployed to the real robot. Randomization is a better approach.

anti_patterns.label: Deploying Pure RL from Scratch
anti_patterns.summary: Deploying pure reinforcement learning from scratch in real-world autonomous systems.
anti_patterns.details: It requires negative examples (like accidents or failures) to learn, making it highly dangerous and time-consuming before the system does anything useful.
anti_patterns.representative_quote: Very few things more fun to watch than a reinforcement learning robot starting from nothing and inventing things. But it's just time consuming and it's not always safe.

anti_patterns.label: Manual Reward Design for Complex Tasks
anti_patterns.summary: Relying on manually designed reward functions for complex, nuanced tasks.
anti_patterns.details: It is hard to define a comprehensive reward for tasks like cooking, which easily leads to 'reward exploitation' where the agent finds unintended loopholes to maximize its score without solving the actual task.

anti_patterns.label: Evaluating AI Solely by Math Complexity
anti_patterns.summary: Judging the value of AI research solely by its mathematical complexity rather than its real-world utility.
anti_patterns.details: This disconnects the research from actual real-world impact and the practical change the work can make.
anti_patterns.representative_quote: ...to really try to see the connection from what you're working on to what impact they can really have, what change it can make rather than what's the math that happened to be in your work.

anti_patterns.label: Ignoring the Perception-Action Loop
anti_patterns.summary: Assuming the supervised learning paradigm applies directly to robotics.
anti_patterns.details: Supervised learning relies on the assumption that one decision does not affect the next request. In robotics, every action changes the subsequent state, requiring reinforcement learning to handle the continuous loop.
